sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A community health survey examined 360 adults served by the same clinic network. Participants were grouped as food secure, marginally food secure, or food insecure. Preventive visit completion during the past year was 74%, 61%, and 43%, respectively. Mean perceived-stress scores on a 1–10 scale were 4.1, 5.8, and 7.6. Interviews suggested that patients with food insecurity were more likely to miss appointments because of unstable work schedules, transportation costs, and competing needs such as rent and groceries.
questions.stem: According to Figure 1, which group had the lowest preventive visit completion?

explanations.A: Correct. The food-insecure group completed preventive visits at 43%, lower than 61% in the marginal group and 74% in the food-secure group. The same group also had the highest stress score, so the data show a clear social gradient in access and strain.
explanations.B: Food-secure adults had the highest preventive visit completion, not the lowest.
explanations.C: The marginal group was intermediate rather than the lowest.
explanations.D: The completion rates differ by more than 30 percentage points across groups.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Preventive visit completion and perceived-stress scores across food-security groups.

questions.stem: The appointment barriers described in the interviews are best understood as examples of what?

explanations.B: Correct. Transportation costs, unstable work schedules, rent pressure, and food access are social and material conditions that shape whether people can obtain care. They are not simply individual preferences; they are upstream determinants that influence health behavior and clinical access.
explanations.A: Fundamental attribution error concerns explaining behavior by overemphasizing traits, whereas the passage highlights structural barriers.
explanations.C: The mere-exposure effect concerns liking after repeated exposure and is not relevant to missed visits.
explanations.D: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced response to constant stimuli, not access to preventive care.

questions.stem: Why would it be inappropriate to conclude that food insecurity alone caused every missed appointment?

choices.C: The study is observational and other correlated hardships could contribute
choices.B: Stress scores were reported on a 1–10 scale
choices.D: The sample used adults rather than children

explanations.C: Correct. Food insecurity was not randomly assigned, and it is likely correlated with transportation, work instability, housing strain, and other stressors. The pattern supports association and a plausible mechanism, but it does not isolate food insecurity as the sole causal factor.
explanations.A: Preventive visit completion is measurable and was recorded in the passage.
explanations.B: A numerical stress scale does not by itself prevent interpretation.
explanations.D: Using adults affects generalizability, not the main causal limitation.

questions.stem: Which clinic intervention would most directly respond to the barriers in the passage?

choices.D: Offering flexible scheduling and transportation support for patients with resource strain
choices.A: Telling patients that missed appointments reflect poor motivation
choices.B: Removing preventive-care reminders from the patient portal
choices.C: Scheduling all patients during weekday work hours only

explanations.D: Correct. Flexible hours and transportation support address the specific constraints named in the interviews. This intervention treats missed visits as partly shaped by resources and schedule constraints rather than as a simple failure of knowledge or motivation.
explanations.A: The interviews identify concrete barriers, so blaming motivation would ignore the mechanism.
explanations.B: Removing reminders would not solve transportation or work-schedule barriers.
explanations.C: Restricting appointments to work hours would likely worsen the stated problem.

questions.stem: Which additional result would most strengthen the interpretation that stress mediates part of the association?

choices.A: Within each food-security group, higher stress scores predicted lower preventive visit completion
choices.B: Food-secure adults had the largest household size
choices.C: The clinic network used one electronic health record
choices.D: Participants liked the survey staff equally

explanations.A: Correct. If stress predicts preventive-care completion within each food-security level, then stress becomes a plausible pathway linking material strain to missed care. That pattern would support mediation rather than merely showing that the groups differ on two unrelated measures.
explanations.B: Household size might be relevant, but it does not specifically test stress as a pathway.
explanations.C: A shared electronic health record standardizes data capture but does not test mediation.
explanations.D: Equal liking of survey staff reduces one response concern but does not explain the stress-care connection.

questions.passage: A mental-health clinic evaluated how stigma concerns affected communication. New patients completed a stigma-concern scale and were grouped into low, moderate, or high concern. During intake, clinicians recorded whether the patient fully disclosed symptoms relevant to diagnosis. Full disclosure rates were 82%, 63%, and 41% across the three stigma groups. Missed follow-up rates were 9%, 17%, and 31%. Patients with high stigma concern often said they feared being judged, labeled, or treated differently by family or employers.
questions.stem: What pattern is shown in Figure 2 as stigma concern increases?

choices.B: Disclosure decreases while missed follow-up increases
choices.A: Disclosure increases while missed follow-up decreases
choices.C: Both disclosure and missed follow-up decrease

explanations.B: Correct. The high-stigma group had the lowest full disclosure rate, 41%, and the highest missed follow-up rate, 31%. Moving from low to high stigma, the two outcomes move in opposite directions, showing less communication and more disengagement.
explanations.A: The figure shows the reverse of this pattern.
explanations.C: Missed follow-up increases rather than decreases as stigma concern rises.
explanations.D: Both outcomes change substantially across groups.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Full symptom disclosure and missed follow-up rates by stigma-concern group.

questions.stem: Fear of being judged or labeled most directly reflects which concept?

choices.C: Stigma as a barrier to help-seeking

explanations.C: Correct. Stigma can discourage people from revealing symptoms or returning for care because they anticipate negative judgment or social consequences. The passage ties stigma concerns directly to disclosure and follow-up behavior, making help-seeking barriers the best interpretation.
explanations.A: Habituation concerns decreased response to repeated stimuli, not fear of social labeling.
explanations.B: Latent learning is learning that occurs without obvious reinforcement and does not explain clinic avoidance.
explanations.D: Role strain can occur in many settings, but the core issue here is stigmatized identity and help-seeking.

questions.stem: Which action would most likely reduce the barrier described in the passage?

choices.D: Using confidentiality explanations and nonjudgmental intake language
choices.A: Asking patients to disclose sensitive symptoms in a crowded waiting room
choices.B: Posting diagnostic labels publicly to normalize them
choices.C: Shortening intake so clinicians avoid symptom questions

explanations.D: Correct. Clear confidentiality protections and nonjudgmental language directly target fear of being judged or exposed. They would make disclosure safer without pressuring patients in ways that intensify stigma concerns.
explanations.A: Public disclosure would likely increase fear and reduce trust.
explanations.B: Public labels could be harmful and would not protect individual privacy.
explanations.C: Avoiding symptom questions would undermine assessment rather than reduce stigma safely.

questions.stem: Why should the clinic avoid interpreting nondisclosure as simple dishonesty?

choices.A: Nondisclosure may reflect anticipated stigma and fear of social consequences
choices.B: Patients with high stigma cannot understand questions
choices.C: Clinicians should never ask follow-up questions
choices.D: Symptoms are irrelevant to mental-health assessment

explanations.A: Correct. The passage suggests that some patients withhold information because disclosure feels socially risky. Interpreting that behavior as dishonesty would miss the psychological and social context that shapes communication.
explanations.B: The passage does not suggest comprehension problems.
explanations.C: Follow-up questions can be helpful when asked sensitively.
explanations.D: Symptoms are central to assessment, which is why disclosure matters.

questions.stem: Which methodological limitation is most important?

choices.B: The stigma groups were based on existing concerns rather than random assignment
choices.A: The study included missed follow-up as an outcome
choices.D: Patients were grouped into three categories

explanations.B: Correct. Because stigma concern was measured rather than manipulated, other factors such as prior discrimination, symptom severity, or social support could contribute to disclosure and follow-up. The findings are meaningful but not fully causal.
explanations.A: Measuring missed follow-up strengthens the outcome assessment rather than creating the main limitation.
explanations.C: Clinician recording is part of the method and not the primary causal issue.
explanations.D: Three categories can summarize the pattern; the key limitation is nonrandom grouping.
